title: Outdoor X Base Table
summary: A gap-top outdoor dining table on a pair of crossing X-shaped legs joined by a center stretcher — simple joinery, built for weather.
description:
This table is built to live outside, and two details matter more than the joinery: leave a 1/4" gap between tabletop boards (a carpenter's pencil laid on edge is a fast, repeatable spacer), and orient every board bark-side down. A flat-sawn board cups away from its bark side as it moves with humidity, so bark-down keeps the top shedding water instead of cupping into a bowl that holds it.

The X-base itself only has one real load path to worry about: the top screws where each X crosses take real leverage whenever the table gets dragged by an end, so a center stretcher between the two X's is what keeps the legs from splitting apart under that load. The source deliberately skips a diagonal cross-brace in favor of a plain 2x2 stretcher, on the reasoning that it's the simpler build for a first-timer — a valid trade worth keeping unless the table will see unusually rough handling.

tools.note: Cuts the 45° apron ends and the compound angles on the X legs.

cutList.note: Ends cut at 45°, long point to long point measurement.
Compound-angle both ends — measure and cut to fit your desired table height.
Finish caps over the leg screw holes.
Measure and cut to fit between the two X's.

steps.title: Cut the apron ends
steps.body: Cut the two apron ends as shown, with 45° angles on the ends. You'll need two of these.

steps.title: Build the table frame
steps.body: Mark the side aprons every 10" (this doesn't need to be exact) and use the marks as a guide to attach supports with 2" screws and glue. Screw the side aprons to the mitered apron ends from the previous step, checking for square as you go.

steps.title: Attach the tabletop boards
steps.body: Fasten the tabletop boards to the frame with 2-1/2" screws and glue. Leave a 1/4" gap between boards (a flat carpenter's pencil works as a spacer) so water can drain, and orient bark-side down on every board to help the top shed water and resist cupping.

steps.title: Build the X-base frames
steps.body: Cut the X-leg pieces with compound angles so each pair crosses evenly, and attach at the center with 2-1/2" self-tapping screws.

steps.title: Finish and attach the legs
steps.body: Attach the leg trim pieces to finish the legs, using 1-1/4" screws and glue, fastening from the insides to hide screw holes. Then fasten the legs to the side aprons with 2-1/2" screws, centering each leg on its apron.

steps.title: Add the center stretcher
steps.body:
Add a 2x2 stretcher from the center of one X to the center of the other, to keep the legs from spreading apart under load. Fasten with 2-1/2" screws, using a level to line up the second end once the first is fastened.

Sand every surface and finish with an exterior-rated product.

images.alt: Outdoor dining table with a crossing X-shaped base
